coredata.dc:title: A novel polymer probe for Zn(II) detection with ratiometric fluorescence signal
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Diao, Haipeng
Guo, Lixia
Liu, Wen
Feng, Liheng
coredata.dc:description: A conjugated polymer probe comprised of fluorene, quinolone and benzothiazole units was designed and synthesized by the Suzuki coupling reaction. Through the studies of photophysical and thermal properties, the polymer displays blue-emitting feature and good thermal stability. A ratiometric fluorescence signal of the probe for Zn(II) was observed in ethanol with a new emission peak at 555 nm. The probe possesses a high selectivity and sensitivity for Zn(II) during familiar metal ions in ethanol. The detection limit of the probe for Zn (II) is up to 10−8 mol/L. The electron distributions of the polymer before and after bonding with Zn (II) were investigated by the Gaussian 09 software, which agreed with the experimental results. Noticeably, based on the color property of the probe with Zn(II), a series of color test paper were developed for visual detecting Zn(II) ions. This work helps to provide a platform or pattern for the development of polymer fluorescence probe in the chemosensor field.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polymer
Zinc ion
Ratiometric
Fluorescence probe
Application

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sensing Behaviors of Polymer PFQT for Metal Ions

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) The fluorescence spectra of PFQT (0.02 mg/mL) with the addition of Zn(II) (from 10−8 mol/L to 10−4 mol/L) in ethanol; b) The color changes of the polymer solution with the increase of Zn (II) ions under the UV365nm light; c) The curve of relative fluorescence intensities of polymer PFQT (0.02 mg/mL) to the concentration of Zn(II) ions; d) The linear relation of relative fluorescence intensities of polymer PFQT (0.02 mg/mL) to the low concentrations of Zn(II) ions.
The fluorescence intensity changes of PFQT (0.02 mg/mL) with various metal ions (5.0 × 10−5 mol/L) in ethanol, respectively. Inset: Solution color changes of polymer (PFQT) with various metal ions.
The bonding mode of polymer PFQT with Zn(II) (left) and the color of the polymer solution (0.02 mg/mL) in the presence of various metal ions (5.0 × 10−5 mol/L), respectively (right).
The designed model of the complex between polymer PFQT and Zn(II) ions, and the electron distributions of HOMO and LUMO orbitals.
Fluorescent paper strips of polymer (PFQT, 0.20 mg/mL) after addition of (a) different concentrations of Zn (II) ions or (b) different metal ions (10−4 M).
The synthesis routes of monomers and polymer (PFQT).

head.ce:title: A novel polymer probe for Zn(II) detection with ratiometric fluorescence signal

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Basic Medical Sciences, Shanxi Medical University, Taiyuan 030001, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan, 030006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The authors of Haipeng Diao and Lixia Guo contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A conjugated polymer probe comprised of fluorene, quinolone and benzothiazole units was designed and synthesized by the Suzuki coupling reaction. Through the studies of photophysical and thermal properties, the polymer displays blue-emitting feature and good thermal stability. A ratiometric fluorescence signal of the probe for Zn(II) was observed in ethanol with a new emission peak at 555 nm. The probe possesses a high selectivity and sensitivity for Zn(II) during familiar metal ions in ethanol. The detection limit of the probe for Zn (II) is up to 10−8 mol/L. The electron distributions of the polymer before and after bonding with Zn (II) were investigated by the Gaussian 09 software, which agreed with the experimental results. Noticeably, based on the color property of the probe with Zn(II), a series of color test paper were developed for visual detecting Zn(II) ions. This work helps to provide a platform or pattern for the development of polymer fluorescence probe in the chemosensor field.

ce:section.ce:para: Recent years, more and more researchers have begun to pay attention to the problem of heavy metal ions [1,2]. Metal ions play an important role in ecological and biological systems [3–5]. Some metal ions are necessary to the physiological balance of human body, while toxic heavy metal ions are able to destroy environment and health even at very low concentration [6–9]. Therefore, there has an increasing interest to detect metal ions in the last few years [10–13]. Zinc, one of the most amount metal ions in biological system, widely exists in pancreas and brain of mammal [14–16]. It is critical for DNA repair, neural signal transmission and gene expression [17,18]. The lack of zinc is associated with infectious diseases, brain trauma, epilepsy, depression, diabetes, Alzheimer disease [19–21]. However, cadmium and zinc are located in the same group of the periodic table with some similar properties [22]. Therefore, it is difficult to achieve good selectivity and high sensitivity for zinc ions [23,24].

Fluorescence analysis has attracted the attention of scientific researchers due to its excellent characteristics of real-time detection, convenient visualization, simple operation, good selectivity and high sensitivity [25–30]. For Zn(II) detection, many small organic fluorescence probes with excellent properties have been reported [31–33]. However, the presence of rapid photo bleaching and low brightness of small molecules seriously hindered the further development as fluorescence probes. The unique structure of conjugated polymers, which possesses plenty of repeat units and long conjugated system, can provide a channel for the efficient transfer of energy, and realize “signal-amplification” [34–40]. So, conjugated polymers exhibit excellent photoelectric properties and thermal stability, and have been widely used in analysis and detection fields [41–44]. Regrettably, many polymer-based fluorescence probes were quenched by metal ions [45,47–50]. It is well known that the concentration, environment and excitation wavelength all effect the fluorescence spectra of probes. For this trouble, ratiometric fluorescence probes can overcome these limitations because they may produce two emission peaks under the same excitation [51,52]. What's more, conjugated polymer-based ratiometric fluorescence probes for metal ions (especially for Zn (II)) are rare at present. So, developing polymer-based ratiometric fluorescence probes with high selectivity and high sensitivity to Zn (II) is significative and desired for chemists and biologists.

Herein, we designed and synthesized a novel conjugated polymer containing quinoline and fluorene units as the main chain, benzothiazole group as the side chain [53]. The intramolecular electron transfer from fluorene and quinoline to benzothiazole units of the polymer resulted in appearing a new emission peak in the presence of Zn(II). Theoretical calculation by Gaussian 09 software further verified the electron transfer process. The conjugated polymer not only has excellent photophysical properties and thermal stability, but also displays high selectivity and sensitivity for Zn(II) with a ratiometric fluorescence response. The color test paper for Zn(II) was a simple and visible application model.

Unless otherwise stated, all chemical reagents were obtained from commercial suppliers and used without further purification. Solvents used were purified and dried by standard methods prior to use. Bis(pinacolato)diboron, Pd(PPh3)4, Pd(dppf)Cl2 were purchased from Aldrich (Steinheim, Germany). Potassium carbonate, 2,7-dibromofluorene, 5,7-dibromo-8-hydroxyquinoline and chloroacetyl chloride benzo[d]thiazol-2-amine were provided by Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Metal ions all were nitrates and obtained from Alfa Aesar (Tianjin, China). 1H NMR and 13C NMR were measured on a Bruker ARX600 spectrometer with chemical shifts reported as ppm (TMS as an internal standard). High-resolution mass spectra (HRMS) were acquired on an Agilent 6510 Q-TOF LC/MS instrument (Agilent Technologies, Palo Alto, CA) equipped with an electrospray ionization (ESI) source. The Perkine Elmer Pyris1 TGA thermal analyzer was used to record the decomposition temperature (Td) corresponding to 5% weight loss. Gel permeation chromatography (GPC) measurements were performed on a Waters-410 system against polystyrene standard with tetrahydrofuran (THF) as eluent. UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectra of the polymer were measured by a Hitachi 5300 absorption and a Hitachi F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer, respectively. The excitation and emission slit widths both were 5.0 nm. The geometries and electron density distributions of the HOMO and LUMO energy levels of the polymer and Zn(II) were calculated by density functional theory (DFT). Gaussian 09 software was used to calculate the DFT/B3LYP/6-31G (d, p) level.

A mixture solution of benzo[d]thiazol-2-amine (3.00 g, 20.0 mmol), chloroacetyl chloride (3.50 g, 30.0 mmol) and potassium carbonate (13.8 g, 0.100 mol) in chloroform (50.0 mL) was refluxed for 12 h. Next, after removed the solvent in vacuo, the residue was washed with 5% NaHCO3 and subsequently with water. The crude product was dried and crystallized from methanol to afford N-(benzo[d]thiazol −2-yl)-2- chloroacetamide (2.82 g, 62.4%). 1H NMR (CDCl3, 600 MHz) δ 12.72 (s, 1H), 8.01 (d, J = 12.00 Hz, 1H), 7.78 (d, J = 12.00 Hz, 1H), 7.45 (m, 1H), 7.33 (m, 1H), 4.47 (s, 2H); 13C NMR (CDCl3, 150 MHz) δ 166.40, 158.06, 131.93, 126.72, 124.28, 122.30, 121.18, 43.01; HRMS-ESI for C9H7ClN2OS (m/z) 226.9971 [M + H].

A mixture solution of N-(benzo[d]thiazol-2-yl)-2-chloroacetamide (0.450 g, 2.00 mmol), 5,7-dibromo-8-hydroxyquinoline (0.300 g, 1.00 mmol), K2CO3 (0.550 g, 4.00 mmol) and KI (50.0 mg, 0.300 mmol) in acetone (60.0 mL) was refluxed for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, the solvent was removed in vacuo and washed three times with water. The residue was purified by column chromatography (dichloromethane/ethyl acetate = 5/1, v/v) to afford N-(benzothiazol-2-yl)-2-((5,7- dibromoquinolin-8-yl) oxy) acetamide (0.220 g, 21.2%). 1H NMR (CDCl3, 600 MHz) δ 14.09 (s, 1H), 9.22 (s, 1H), 8.62 (d, J = 6.60 Hz, 1H), 8.33 (d, J = 7.20 Hz, 1H), 8.02 (d, J = 6.60 Hz, 1H), 7.91 (t, J = 6.60, 12.00 Hz, 1H) 7.87 (d, J = 6.60 Hz, 1H), 7.49 (t, J = 6.60, 7.20 Hz, 1H), 7.35 (t, J = 6.60, 7.20 Hz, 1H), 5.22 (s, 2H); 13C NMR (CDCl3, 150 MHz) δ 168.80, 158.03, 152.31, 151.59, 149.13, 142.50, 137.03, 133.97, 132.03, 127.91, 126.71, 124.57, 124.23, 122.34, 121.34, 116.93, 116.26, 73.50; HRMS-ESI for C18H11Br2N3O2S (m/z) 493.8912 [M + H].

A mixture of 50% potassium hydroxide solution (25.0 mL), 1,6-dibromohexane (24.4 g, 0.100 mol), tetrabutylammonium bromide (TBAB) (0.320 g) was heated to 75 °C. Then 2,7-dibromofluorene (3.24 g, 10.0 mmol) was added into the above solution and stirred for 6 h. Next, the reaction mixture was cooled to room temperature, extracted with dichloromethane and washed with water. The combined organic layer was dried over anhydrous MgSO4, After removing a mass of dichloromethane, the residue was purified by column chromatography (petroleum ether/dichloromethane = 9/1, v/v) to afford monomer 1 [9,9-bis(6′-bromohexyl)-2,7-di bromofluorene] (4.96 g, 78.1%). 1H NMR: (600 MHz, CDCl3, ppm) δ: 0.57 (m, 4H), 1.15 (m, 4H), 1.22 (m, 4H), 1.54 (m, 4H), 1.85 (m, 4H), 3.36 (m, 4H), 7.43 (s, 4H), 7.50 (s, 2H); 13C NMR: (150 MHz, CDCl3, ppm) δ: 152.14, 139.22, 130.38, 126.12, 121.25, 55.21, 40.15, 33.89, 32.71, 29.29, 27.76, 23.24. HRMS-ESI for C25H30Br4 (m/z): 650.9048 [M + H].

A mixture solution of 9,9-bis(6′-bromohexyl)-2,7-dibromofluorene (0.650 g, 1.00 mmol), bis(pinacolato)diboron (0.740 g, 3.00 mmol), Pd(dppf)Cl2 (15.0 mg) and potassium acetate (0.350 g, 3.60 mmol) in DMF (20.0 mL) was stirred at 100 °C for 12 h under nitrogen atmosphere. The resulting reaction solution was poured into water (100 mL), extracted with dichloromethane and washed with water for two times, respectively. The combined organic layer was dried over anhydrous MgSO4. After removing a mass of dichloromethane, the residue was purified by column chromatography (petroleum ether/ethyl acetate = 5/1, v/v) to afford monomer 2 2,2′-(9,9-bis(6-bromohexyl)-9H–fluorene-2,7-diyl)bis(4,4,5,5-tetra-methyl-1,3,2-dioxaborolane) (0.340 g, 42.2%). 1H NMR (600 MHz, CDCl3): δ 7.83–7.72 (m, 6H), 3.27 (t, J = 7.20, 6.60 Hz, 4H), 2.03–1.99 (m, 4H), 1.64–1.57 (m, 4H), 1.39 (s, 24H), 1.17–1.13 (m, 4H), 1.06–1.02 (m, 4H), 0.55 (m, 4H). 13C NMR (150 MHz, CDCl3): δ 150.21, 144.12, 133.95, 128.98, 119.72, 83.89, 55.20, 40.15, 34.12, 32.88, 29.17, 27.92, 23.56. HRMS-ESI for C37H54B2Br2O4 (m/z): 745.2561 [M + H].

A mixture solution of monomer 1 (0.100 g, 0.200 mmol), monomer 2 (0.130 g, 0.200 mmol) and 2.00 mol/L Na2CO3 (3.00 mL) in DMF (15.0 mL) and was stirred for 30 min under nitrogen atmosphere. Then, 15.0 mg Pd(PPh3)4 catalyst was quickly added to the suspension and heated the mixture to 110 °C for 48 h under nitrogen atmosphere. The resulting suspension was concentrated to 2.00–3.00 mL and poured into 50.0 mL chloroform. The obtained mixture was washed with water for three times. The combined organic layer was dried over anhydrous MgSO4. After removal of a mass of chloroform, the residue was precipitated with methanol and the precipitate was collected by centrifugation. The crude polymer was reprecipitated with methanol twice to afford polymer PFQT (46.0 mg, 27.2%). 1H NMR (600 MHz, CDCl3): δ 13.28 (s, 1H), 8.65–8.34 (b, 2H), 8.21–8.02 (b, 4H), 7.88–7.45 (b, 8H), 4.89 (s, 2H), 3.34 (t, J = 12.00, 6.60 Hz, 4H), 1.92 (m, 8H), 1.22 (m, 12H). 13C NMR (150 MHz, CDCl3): δ 166.72, 159.23, 153.34, 151.46, 144.72, 142.53, 138.13, 132.86, 128.98, 127.71, 126.64, 125.17, 124.43, 123.11, 121.46, 118.32, 72.16, 53.27, 42.25, 36.12, 33.78, 30.07, 28.22, 24.36. Mn = 13,945, Mw = 19,245, PDI = 1.38.

The stock solution of polymer was 0.100 mg/mL in ethanol. The working solutions were prepared by diluting method. The working solution (20.0 μg/mL) was placed in a quartz cuvette with 1 cm path. The total volume of working solution was 2.00 mL. The stock solutions of metal ions in redistilled water were all 0.010 mol/L. The fluorescence measurements used titration experiments and the volume added did not exceed 3% of the total. After shaken for 30 s, the new spectra were measured. All of the experiments were performed at barometric pressure and room temperature.

The specification filter paper was soaked in ethanol solution of the polymer for 1.0 min. Inhere, the concentration of the polymer was 20.0 μg/mL. Then, the filter paper with polymer was placed in room temperature and removed the solvent.

The synthesis routes of monomers and polymer (PFQT) were displayed in Scheme 1 . The synthesis of monomer 1 was to connect benzothiazole and quinoline via chloroacetyl chloride. Monomer 2 could be obtained by a two-step reaction of 2,7-dibromofluorene with a higher yield. Polymer (PFQT) was prepared by the typical Suzuki coupling method between monomer 1 and monomer 2 in the presence of Pd catalyst. The structures of monomer 1, monomer 2 and polymer were characterized by 1H NMR, 13C NMR and MS-ESI, respectively.

The UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectra were used to investigate the photophysical properties of polymer PFQT in ethanol (Table S1). As can be seen from Table S1, the two absorption peaks of polymer PFQT are located at 318 nm and 487 nm, respectively. The emission wavelength of PFQT is 400 nm under 340 nm excitation, which emits a blue light. Compared to two monomers, the photophysical properties of PFQT are close to that of monomer 2, which displays the properties of PFQT mainly come from monomer 2. The fluorescent quantum yield of PFQT in ethanol is 0.62 based on quinine sulfate in 0.10 M sulfuric acid as the reference, which the fluorescent quantum yield is 0.54. In order to assay the thermal stability of PFQT, thermal gravimetric analysis (TGA) was utilized under nitrogen atmosphere. The decomposition temperature (Td, 5% weight loss) of PFQT is about 207 °C (Fig. S1). The result indicates that the polymer PFQT exhibits a good thermal stability because of its large rigid structure and conjugated extent [53].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing Behaviors of Polymer PFQT for Metal Ions
ce:section.ce:para:
As designed, the sensing behaviors of PFQT for various metal ions (Zn2+, K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Cd2+, Al3+, Fe2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Cr3+, Ag+, Mn2+, Ni2+ and Co2+) were investigated by fluorescence technique in ethanol (Figs. 1 and 2
), respectively. An new peak at 555 nm of polymer PFQT appears with the introduction of Zn(II) ions. Meanwhile, the emission peak at 400 nm of PFQT has no obvious change (Fig. 1a). The case displays that the polymer as a probe has a ratiometric fluorescence signal for Zn(II) ions. As can be seen from Fig. 2, the ratiometric response signal of polymer PFQT is observed by only adding Zn(II) during various metal ions. Interestingly, the color of the polymer solutions change from original blue to yellow in the presence of Zn(II) under 365 nm UV lamp (Fig. 2). So, the ratiometric response specificity of polymer PFQT for Zn(II) is embodied by the above assays. To further explore the sensing behaviors of the polymer to Zn(II), the fluorescence of PFQT was carefully investigated with the gradual increase of Zn(II) (Fig. 1). From Fig. 1a, the increase of 555 nm peak and the slight reduction at 400 nm are obviously observed simultaneously. Correspondingly, the color of PFQT solution gradually changes from blue to yellow with the increase of Zn(II) under the UV lamp (Fig. 1b). The curve of the relative intensities (F555nm/F400nm) of PFQT to the concentration of Zn (II) was shown in Fig. 1c. However, there has a good linear relation for the sensing process at low concentration of Zn(II) (<1.0 × 10−5 mol/L) (Fig. 1d). The detection limit of PFQT for Zn (II) is up to 5.0 × 10−8 mol/L. These results indicate the polymer PFQT has a high selectivity and sensitivity for Zn (II) as a ratiometric fluorescence probe.

In order to obtain the stoichiometric of the complex between polymer PFQT and Zn (II), the Job's plot assay was performed (Fig. S2). As shown in Fig. S2, the maximum intensity of fluorescence appears at the mole ratio of 5/5, which indicates that the bonding ratio of PFQT with Zn(II) is the 1/1 mol mode. Based on the stoichiometry, the bonding mode of PFQT with Zn (II) was proposed and listed in Fig. 3 . As shown from Fig. 3, the N and O atoms of the molecule and ethanol solvent are involved in the bonding mode. In the polymer, quinoline and fluorene units are both electron donors, benzothiazole acts as an electron acceptor. Therefore, before the addition of Zn (II), the emission peak of PFQT mainly derives from fluorene group because it has higher fluorescence, and there has no charge transfer from quinoline to benzothiazole units [54–56]. After introducing Zn(II), the intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) from donors to acceptor takes place due to the complexing action of Zn(II) with N and O atoms. The ICT action of polymer FQT and Zn(II) leads to the appearance of a new peak, and the color change of polymer solution from blue to yellow also is observed. To verify the assumption, the interactions of the two monomers and Zn (II) also were studied (Fig. S3), respectively. As expected, the fluorescence of monomers has no similar change with the addition of Zn(II) ions. Although the monomer 1 possesses a similar structure with polymer PFQT, the fluorescence of monomer 1 has no the same change with the introduction of Zn (II) because the electron-withdrawing group (-Br) of monomer 1 inhibits the ICT process.

For further validate the bonding mode, the electron distributions of the polymer before and after complexing with Zn (II) were calculated. The model chemistry was employed to build the structure of PFQT, and optimized by the density functional theory (DFT/B3LYP/6-31G (d, p)). The electron densities of the HOMO and LUMO for PFQT were shown in Fig. 4 . From Fig. 4, the electrons of the polymer in the LUMO orbit are mainly concentrated in the fluorene moiety. However, the electrons distribute in the quinoline moiety after combined with Zn(II). The energy gaps between the LUMO and HOMO of PFQT and PFQT/Zn(II) are calculated as 3.94 eV and 2.66 eV, respectively (Fig. S4). The result shows that the complexation of Zn (II) with the polymer PFQT reduces the energy gap of the complex and stabilizes the system.

For the practical application, it is important to develop a simple, portable and inexpensive paper sensor [57,58]. Herein, a good selective and polymer-based ratiometric fluorescent probe was designed as a Zn(II)-responsive paper sensor, which can be achieved by visualizing fluorescence detection of Zn(II). The prepared filter paper firstly was placed in ethanol solution of the probe, followed by air-drying. And then separately dropping different concentrations of Zn (II) ions or other metal ions on the filters, the response signal was observed under UV365nm lamp. As shown in Fig. 5a, with the increase of Zn (II), the color of the test paper gradually changed from blue to light-green under UV365nm light. The color of the test paper changed significantly under only 10−6 mol/L concentration of Zn (II) ions. Beside the sensitivity, the selectivity of the probe paper for Zn(II) also was investigated (Fig. 5b). When the addition of different kinds of metal ions, Zn(II) ions can lead to significant fluorescence change in the test strip with a litter interference from Cd(II) in the higher concentration (Fig. 5b).

In summary, we designed and synthesized a polymer-based ratiometric fluorescence probe with fluorene and quinoline as the main chain and benzothiazole group in the side chain. In the polymer, the Br group on the alkyl end as a linked site may obtain branchlike functional compounds in the future. The polymer possesses excellent photophysical and thermal properties. Based on the intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) mechanism, the probe exhibits high selectivity and high sensitivity for Zn (II) with an obvious ratiometric response. Importantly, the ratiometric fluorescent probe can be designed as a Zn(II)-responsive paper-based sensor, which will provide a simple and low cost assay method for metal ions in practical samples.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work described in this paper was supported by the National Nature Science Foundation (No. 21571116 and 21601111), the Youth Science Foundation of Shanxi Province (No. 201601D021054), Shanxi Science and Technology Development Program (Grant No. 201703D321015-2), SanJin Scholars Support Plan under Special Funding (No. 2017-06), and the Program for the Outstanding Innovative Teams of Higher Learning Institutions of Shanxi (No. 2017-07). We also thank the Scientific Instrument Center of Shanxi University for the assistance during the characterization of the compounds.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Other fluorescence spectroscopy (PDF) associated with this article can be found in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2018.02.036.

The molecular wire approach to sensory signal amplification
Charge transport in intermixed regions of all-polymer solar cells studied by conductive atomic force microscopy
A novel blue emission PLED material: synthesis, photophysical and electrochemical properties

Influence of electron-donor-acceptor complexation on electronic relaxation of quinoline
Density functional theory studies of new bipolar carbazole-benzothiazole: electronic and vibrational properties
Fluorescence “turn on” detection of mercuric ion based on bis(dithiocarbamato) copper(II) complex functionalized carbon nanodots
A new fluorescent pyrene–pyridine dithiocarbamate probe: a chemodosimeter to detect Hg2+ in pure aqueous medium and in live cells
